  "opinion": "As a retired man from Naples who grew up in a time when science was largely dominated by men, I've observed the gender gap in science as a slow, persistent divide that's more about societal expectations than innate ability. My parents, one with no formal education and the other with a vocational diploma, taught me that hard work and curiosity mattered most, not gender. Back in my youth, I saw few girls in engineering or physics classes, but I also remember a girl in my neighborhood who loved solving puzzles and building things with her hands—she was always encouraged to be creative, even though she didn't go on to study science. Today, the gap is still there, but it's not as stark as it was in my day. The key isn't to force girls into science or boys out of it, but to create spaces where everyone feels they belong. I've spent my retirement reading novels and cooking, which has helped me stay grounded, but I can't help but think about how much more we could achieve if we stopped treating science as a 'boys' club' and started seeing it as a shared pursuit. With my depression and low conscientiousness, I know that change takes time and patience, but I believe we must address this gap with kindness, not pressure. The solution lies in early education that celebrates all talents equally, not in trying to change people's genders but in changing how we see them. After all, science belongs to all of us, not just one gender.",

response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a retired man from Naples who grew up in a time when science was largely dominated by men, I've observed the gender gap in science as a slow, persistent divide that's more about societal expectations than innate ability. My parents, one with no formal education and the other with a vocational diploma, taught me that hard work and curiosity mattered most, not gender. Back in my youth, I saw few girls in engineering or physics classes, but I also remember a girl in my neighborhood who loved solving puzzles and building things with her hands—she was always encouraged to be creative, even though she didn't go on to study science. Today, the gap is still there, but it's not as stark as it was in my day. The key isn't to force girls into science or boys out of it, but to create spaces where everyone feels they belong. I've spent my retirement reading novels and cooking, which has helped me stay grounded, but I can't help but think about how much more we could achieve if we stopped treating science as a 'boys' club' and started seeing it as a shared pursuit. With my depression and low conscientiousness, I know that change takes time and patience, but I believe we must address this gap with kindness, not pressure. The solution lies in early education that celebrates all talents equally, not in trying to change people's genders but in changing how we see them. After all, science belongs to all of us, not just one gender.
